coredata.dc:title: Nanopaper-based photoluminescent enantioselective sensing of L-Lysine by L-Cysteine modified carbon quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Copur, Funda
Bekar, Nisa
Zor, Erhan
Alpaydin, Sabri
Bingol, Haluk
coredata.dc:description: The production and application of chiral nanomaterials are expanding to understand the nature of chiral interactions and to detect chiral molecules due to the chirality is a fundamental characteristics of life processes. With the new advances in optical sensors by combining the advantages of optical signaling unit and sensing platform, new avenues can be opened to produce simple and useful devices for chiral sensor applications. Here, we report construction of nanoscale chirality of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by functionalization with L-cysteine and application for paper-based sensors. Within this respect, nanopaper made of cellulose nanofibers was prepared and the produced chiral carbon quantum dots (cCQDs) were embedded into nanopaper to gain photoluminescence properties. The chiral sensing behavior of cCQDs was investigated in solution phase and nanopaper. cCQDs exhibited an enantioselective response towards L-lysine among the tested enantiomers of amino acids with a LOD value of 0.30 mM and 0.97 mM for solution phase and nanopaper, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral recognition
Chiral carbon quantum dots
Nanopaper
Paper-based sensor
L-lysine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of L-cysteine modified carbon quantum dots and nanopaper
Enantioselective optical sensing performance of cCQDs in solution phase
Application of cCQDs-incorporated nanopaper for photoluminescent chiral sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematized synthesis and application procedures.

FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs and cCQDs (A) The full range XPS spectra of N-CQDs (B) and cCQDs (C) The high resolution C1s XPS spectra of N-CQDs (D) and cCQDs (E) The high resolution S2p XPS spectrum of cCQDs (F).
HR-TEM image of N-CQDs (A) and cCQDs (B) AFM images of N-CQDs (C) and cCQDs (D).
PL spectra of N-CQDs (A) and cCQDs (B). The corresponding photos show N-CQDs (C) and cCQDs (D) solutions taken under day and UV light (365 nm).

PL spectrum changes of cCQDs (0.1 mg/mL) with Lys enantiomers (A) PL titration experiments of cCQDs in aqueous media upon the increasing amount of L-Lys (1.5–20 mM) (B). The inset exhibits the corresponding calibration curve at 424 nm.
PL spectra of cCQDs in the presence of enantiomeric percentage of L-Lys. The inset shows the corresponding curve between I/Io and the percentage of L-Lys (10–100%).
The color change under UV light as a function of D-Lys and L-Lys quantity (0.0–20 mM).
The color change of cCQDs embedded NPs as a function of D-Lys and L-Lys concentration.
The possible structure of cCQDs (A). The conformational modeling for the interaction between D-Lys and cCQDs (B), and L-Lys and cCQDs (C) from different chiral centers (regions 1, 2 and 3).
Gibbs free energies for D- and L-Lys docking on cCQDs from different regions.

head.ce:title: Nanopaper-based photoluminescent enantioselective sensing of L-Lysine by L-Cysteine modified carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Institute of Science, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Nanoscience and Nanoengineering, Institute of Science, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science Education, A. K. Education Faculty, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry Education, A. K. Education Faculty, Necmettin Erbakan University, Konya, 42090, Turkey

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The production and application of chiral nanomaterials are expanding to understand the nature of chiral interactions and to detect chiral molecules due to the chirality is a fundamental characteristics of life processes. With the new advances in optical sensors by combining the advantages of optical signaling unit and sensing platform, new avenues can be opened to produce simple and useful devices for chiral sensor applications. Here, we report construction of nanoscale chirality of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by functionalization with L-cysteine and application for paper-based sensors. Within this respect, nanopaper made of cellulose nanofibers was prepared and the produced chiral carbon quantum dots (cCQDs) were embedded into nanopaper to gain photoluminescence properties. The chiral sensing behavior of cCQDs was investigated in solution phase and nanopaper. cCQDs exhibited an enantioselective response towards L-lysine among the tested enantiomers of amino acids with a LOD value of 0.30 mM and 0.97 mM for solution phase and nanopaper, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Chiral nanomaterials (CNMs) have the potential to revolutionize the enantioselective production/separation and the determination of enantiomers of a chiral compound with simple and disposable sensing systems [1,2]. CNMs can be constructed using metal nano-crystals or, particularly, semiconductor quantum dots stabilized by stereoselective chiral molecules which have also opened new avenues of interest [3]. Chiral semiconductor quantum dots have become well-established photoluminescent nanomaterials [4–7]. However, the majority of the researches focused on the cadmium-based quantum dots which exhibit toxic effect on biological systems and lead serious environmental problems. Apart from these hazardous nanomaterials, carbon-based quantum dots, which do not possess any toxic class of elements, have recently attracted substantial research interest with their outstanding merits such as full-color luminescence, stability and biocompatibility [8,9]

Similar to other fields in nanotechnology, graphene and related materials as carbon-based nanostructures have greatly enriched properties of chiral nanostructures and facilitated their applications [10–12]. In this respect, Suzuki et al. have reported D-(or L-)cysteine-linked chiral graphene quantum dots (cGQDs) through a top-down process using carbon fibers [11]. In their study, chirality originates from L/D-cysteine moieties bound to the edges of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) that leads to their helical buckling due to chiral interactions. Alike to their study, Vazquez-Nakagawa et al. followed a similar top-down procedure by fragmentation of graphite in strong acidic media to produce of GQDs and then reacted with (R) or (S)-2-phenyl-1-propanol to produce cGQDs [13].

Bottom-up method can be performed to construct carbon quantum dots CQDs which are small-sized carbon nanostructures (less than 10 nm) with tunable luminescence and high quantum yields over GQDs [14–17]. The advances and availability of powerful structural elucidation techniques have endowed the discovery of new CQDs architectures [18–20]. Although research on different forms of CQDs is abundant, chirality of CQDs and their optical enantioselective sensing performance have been scarcely investigated. Therefore, a study on CQDs with controlled luminescent would be beneficial for obtaining CQDs with strong emission and for application in various areas such as disease diagnosis, medical research, bioanalysis and (bio)sensor technology for the identification and detection of chiral species owing to their vital importance in living systems.

As building blocks of proteins, amino acids (AAs) form an important class of biological molecules. It is well known that AAs exist in the L-form in nature and D-form sometimes denotes a negative symptom or metabolism abnormality. [21–23]. Therefore, the detection of D- or L-form is of great importance [24]. To illustrate, L-lysine (L-Lys) is an essential amino acid and has a key biological role in central nervous system. On the other hand, abnormal metabolism of L-Lys involves damage of liver function [24]. Taking into account these serious affects, enantioselective sensing of Lys enantiomers is considered to be an important issue to investigate. Different methods, such as chemiluminescence method [25], electrochemical [26], gas chromatography [27] and liquid chromatography [28], have been previously reported for detection of L-Lys. In recent years, fluorescence method has attracted great interests of researchers, due to its advantages such as fast response, high sensitivity and easy operation [24].

The combination of chirality with optical activeness and printable/designable feature on solid platforms can endow nanomaterials a new ability for discriminative sensing of enantiomers not only in solution phase but also on solid phase. However, most of optical nanomaterials are not suitable to make paper-based sensors due to the loss of optical activity at the dry states, the difficulty of transfer on solid substrates, and the limit of sensing mechanism [29]. For this reason, the use of cCQDs in the optical chiral sensors, particularly in disposable paper-based sensors, would be highly desired to explore for the detection of chiral species.

Accordingly, we herein examine a new concept using cCQDs, thanks to their superior physical properties, which are expected to be used as highly efficient chiral fluorescent nanomaterials, and we show that this phenomena should be regarded as an extraordinarily valuable asset for chiral analysis on paper-based platform. Thus, to provide answers to each of the above issues, we propose cCQDs to obtain paper-based chiral sensors for versatile visualization assays. The synthesis of cCQDs was performed with a mixture of ethylenediamine and L-cysteine. The purified cCQDs were characterized by UV–vis, FT-IR, XPS, PL, AFM and TEM techniques. The resultant cCQDs showed different enantioselective response toward L-Lys among the tested enantiomers of amino acids due to conformational difference. In addition, this phenomena was elucidated by molecular modeling studies.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), citric acid (CA) and D-/L-enantiomers of the used chiral analytes (alanine, arginine, asparagine, aspartic acid, cysteine, cystine, histidine, leucine, lysine, methionine, phenylalanine, proline, serine, threonine, tryptophan, tyrosine, valine) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) images were taken on a Tecnai G2-F30 FEI. Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images were taken on a Park XE7 instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on Perkin Elmer 100 FTIR in the 550 − 4000 cm−1 range in ambient air at room temperature. The photoluminescent spectra were recorded on a PTI QuantaMaster™40. A teflon-lined autoclave was used for the synthesis of cCQDs. Ultrapure water was prepared by a Milli-Q system (Millipore).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of L-cysteine modified carbon quantum dots and nanopaper
ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent chiral group modified carbon quantum (cCQDs) dots were obtained in two steps. In the first step, taking into account our previous study [30] to obtain high quantum yield carbon dots, 0.5 g citric acid and 0.285 g ethylenediamine were mixed, and it was heated to 160 °C for 4 h. Afterwards, the color of the melting mixture turned from colorless to pale brown. Then, the resultant nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) solution was diluted to 50 mL and was purified in the dialysis bag (MWCO: 0.5 kDa) for a short time. In the second step, in order to gain chiral feature of N-CQDs, a 5 mL of N-CQDs solution was added into jacketed round-bottom flask and stirred at 0 °C after addition of 200 mg of EDC for half an hour. Then, 100 mg of NHS was added and stirred for 3 h. Finally, an excess amount (100 mg) of L-cysteine was added and stirred for 5 h. The resultant was stirred at ambient temperature for overnight. The final solution was purified in the dialysis bag (MWCO: 0.5 kDa). Bacterial cellulose nanopaper was fabricated according to our previous papers [30,31]. The overall synthesis and application procedures was schematized in Scheme 1 A and B, respectively.

AutoDock Vina (ADVina) was employed as computational method to confirm the binding mode and interaction energy between D-/L-Lys and cCQDs [32]. PDB files of D- and L-Lys structures were taken from ChemSpider (CSS Codes: 51,793 and 5747, respectively). Discovery Studio v4.0 was used to draw the possible cCQD structure according to previously reported article [33] and energetically minimized. Aiming at optimizing the receptor and ligand from the pdb files, AutoDockTools (ADT, version 1.5.6) was employed to supplement Gasteiger charges and polar hydrogen atoms, and to arrange rotatable bonds prior to the docking process. ADT was also used to export files in pdbqt format required for ADVina. D- and L-Lys enantiomers were docked on three different regions at the edges of cCQD scaffold employing the cartesian coordinates of x = 6.140 Å, y=-7.000 Å, z = 7.085 Å with a grid box size of 8 × 10 x 10 Å (x, y and z axes) for region 1, x=-13.440 Å, y = 0.000 Å, z = 0.700 Å with a grid box size of 8 × 10 x 10 Å (x, y and z axes) for region 2, x = 3.880 Å, y = 9.780 Å, z = 2.990 Å with a grid box size of 8 × 10 x 10 Å (x, y and z axes) for region 3 using a grid spacing of 1.000 Å. Gibbs free energy change (ΔGbind) of binding was estimated using the data of ADVina scores.

The structural changes obtained by the preparation of cCQDs via covalent modification of N-CQDs were investigated by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and compared to N-CQDs. Fig. 1 A shows the FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs and cCQDs. The characteristic absorption bands were observed at 2969-2858, 1715 and 1552 cm−1 which correspond to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO and CC, respectively [34,35]. The different peaks at 1395 − 1022 cm−1 are ascribed to CN and CO stretching and COH bending vibrations. Both spectra also contains broad absorption bands at 3400–3050 cm−1, which is caused by the stretching vibrations of NH and OH [36]. After covalently functionalization of N-CQDs with L-Cys, not only the stretching vibration band of SH appeared at 2598 cm−1 but also the intensity of typical peak at 1632 correspond to the stretching modes of CO in amid group increased whereas the stretching modes of CO in carbonyl group (1715 cm−1) slightly decreased [37]. It can be attributed to the formation of the additional amide linkage between N-CQDs and L-Cys, which reveals the successful bonding of L-Cys groups into the N-CQDs moiety.

XPS technique was further performed for surface characterization of N-CQDs and cCQDs. The full range XPS spectra of N-CQDs and cCQDs were respectively shown in Fig. 1B and C. C1s, N1s and O1s signals were observed around 284, 400 and 531 eV. For cCQDs, an additional peak around 164.5 eV was detected. It was assigned to S2p, which confirmed the successful functionalization process of N-CQDs with L-Cys to obtain cCQDs. According to the XPS analysis, the atomic percentages of the main elements in the N-CQDs were obtained as 74.63% (C), 16.75% (O), and 6.01% (N), whereas 66.02% (C), 17.86% (O), 12.05% (N) and 2.81% (S) for cCQDs.

Fig. 1D shows the de-convoluted C1s spectrum of N-CQDs. Similar to the previous literature [30,38], CC, CC, CN, CO, CO/CN and OCO were observed at 283.6, 284.4, 285.0, 285.9, 287.8 and 288.6 eV. In addition, CC, CC, CN, CO, CO/CN and OCO were obtained at 283.4, 284.5, 285.4, 286.1, 287.6 and 288.8 eV in the C1s spectrum of cCQDs (Fig. 1E). As shown in Fig. 1F, the XPS spectrum of S2p displayed two major de-convoluted peaks at 163.2 and 164.4 eV that correspond to the thiol group (SH) of L-Cys molecule and CS covalent bond [39,40], respectively. These overall results structurally confirmed that cCQDs were synthesized by the amidation reaction between the amine groups of L-Cys and the carboxylic groups of N-CQDs.

TEM and AFM techniques were used for morphological characterization of both carbon quantum dots. Fig. 2 A shows HR-TEM micrograph of the well dispersed N-CQDs, which are uniform with an average diameter around 7 nm. Fig. 2B shows HR-TEM micrograph of cCQDs with an average diameter about 10 nm. The chemical modification of N-CQDs with L-Cys on carboxyl groups at the edge led to size growth in the lateral direction. These results provided an evidence of carbon quantum dots homogenously dispersed in aqueous media without aggregation. In addition, Fig. 2C and D show AFM images of N-CQDs and cCQDs which were prepared by respectively casting and drying suspension on a mica substrate. In consistent with the TEM results, the AFM micrographs also confirmed that the nanoparticles were uniform-flattened spheres and possessed similar particle heights.

To analyze the optical properties of N-CQDs and cCQDs, the photoluminescence excitation and emission spectra were recorded and given in Fig. 3 A and B, respectively. The excitation spectrum of N-CQDs showed two bands centered at 302 and 362 nm. The first absorption band at 302 nm was attributed to n-π* transition from aromatic sp2 domains [40], while the other generated by trapping of excited surface state energy which resulted strong emission positioned at 445 nm. As can be seen in the spectra of cCQDs, the emission peak centered at 424 nm was obtained when the excitation wavelength was set at 314 nm. The obtained lower excitation wavelength can be attributed to sulphur content originated from the incorporated L-Cys to N-CQDs, which can trap more excitons leading to the violet emission [41]. The photographs Fig. 3C and D show N-CQDs and cCQDs solutions taken under day and UV light, which confirm the strong blue color luminescence of both nanomaterials. The quantum yields of N-CQDs and cCQDs in aqueous solutions were determined to be 69.5% and 16.1% using quinine sulphate as a standard reference (54% in 0.5 M H2SO4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enantioselective optical sensing performance of cCQDs in solution phase
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots have been reported as convenient receptors with ﬂuorescence signaling modes for chiral sensing applications [42–47]. Accordingly, as initial demonstration of the performance of cCQDs as photoluminescent enantioselective sensor, the classical photoluminescence measurements (PL) were performed in solution. The principle depends on a photoluminescent intensity change upon addition of analyte, which is a simple but efficient way widely used previously. Correspondingly, the solutions including excess concentration of the tested chiral species were spiked into the solution of cCQDs (0.1 mg/mL) and PL spectra were recorded in Britton–Robinson buffer solution at pH 9.5, which was the most suitable pH value to follow the enantioselective response and the change in PL intensity (see Fig. S1 for optical response at different pH values). Fig. S2 shows the PL responses of cCQDs toward 17 kinds of amino acids. It is easily seen that only L-Lys could remarkably increase the PL intensity of cCQDs among the tested amino acids indicating that cCQDs showed an enantioselective response towards L-Lys. Moreover, it should be remarked that both antipodes of the rest of the tested amino acids did not display any PL response difference, which depicted that both antipodes act in the same manner. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the addition of L-Lys led to an enhancement in the PL intensity whereas almost no significant response was obtained for D-Lys. It was attributed to the dimensional position between cCQDs and the chiral species, and steric hindrance effecting the interaction. The emission enhancement can be assigned to the restriction of intra-molecular vibration and rotation of functionalized units as the result of the interaction between L-Lys and cCQDs, which reduces the non-radiative relaxation of excited states [48–50]. It should be remarked that differently from the previously reported researches [24,51] at which PL recovery was followed after quenching by metal ions which would made the sensing phenomena more complicated by enhancing uncertainty in assessment of the whole process, we directly investigated the change in PL intensity without the need of any other indicator.

Aiming at investigating the PL change as a function of L-Lys quantity, we performed a series of PL measurements and the results were presented in Fig. 4B. Correspondingly, the PL peak at 424 nm (exc. 314 nm) gradually increased with the increasing concentration of L-Lys. The relative PL intensities (I/I0) were plotted against the concentration of L-Lys. Considering the corresponding calibration curve in the inset of Fig. 4B, LOD and LOQ values were respectively calculated as 0.3 and 0.9 mM with a wide linear detection range of 0.0–20.0 mM. As an important issue, the enantioselective sensing capability of cCQDs was examined by adjusting enantiomeric percentage. Fig. 5 shows the dependence of the PL intensity upon the enantiomeric percentage of L-Lys. As seen in the inset of Fig. 5, PL intensity enhanced as a function of enantiomeric ratio of L-Lys, which evidenced that enantiomeric percentage could be determined.

Our second demonstration for PL response towards Lys enantiomers was to employ cCQDs solution as an optical probe under UV light. To this aim, PL emission were observed by naked eye for different D- and L-Lys concentrations. Fig. 6 shows the appreciable emission intensity increment from light blue to strong blue for increasing concentration of L-Lys. However, PL change was not observed in the case of D-Lys indicating that cCQDs enantioselectively interacted with one enantiomer of Lys. As clearly seen, the increment in color intensity under UV light depends on the concentration of L-Lys, as evidenced by the PL spectra above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of cCQDs-incorporated nanopaper for photoluminescent chiral sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Bearing in mind the enantioselectivity performance of cCQDs, we foresaw to develop a simple disposable paper-based L-Lys selective sensor for practical applications. To this aim, we utilized cCQDs-embedded nanopaper as an assay for enantioselective sensing of L-Lys. In our previous research [30], we showed that blank nanopaper was optically transparent and could not emit under UV light. Taking into account this remarkably important property, we could incorporate carbon quantum dots into nanopaper to utilize in optical sensing. Similar to our previous research [30], we herein fabricated quantum dots embedded nanopapers by sampling cCQDs solution onto prepared circular pieces of nanopaper. The performance of cCQDs embedded nanopapers was assessed by measuring the PL intensity under UV light. Within this respect, cCQDs embedded nanopapers were cut in circular pieces and treated with the different concentration of L-Lys by sampling (See Fig. 7 ).

As seen in Fig. 7A and B by naked eye, PL of cCQDs embedded nanopapers gradually increased upon addition of increasing concentration of L-Lys (0.0 − 5.0 mM) as observed in solution phase experiments. PL intensity remained stable after 5.0 mM of L-Lys addition which was attributed to the saturation of the response.

For quantitative assessment, the color density of each nanopaper is converted to the color intensity (CI) by employing ImageJ software. Fig. 7C shows the change of color intensity upon concentration (C) increase of L-Lys between 1 mM and 5.0 mM. As experimental evidences, CI value linearly increases (R2 = 0.9979) that can be estimated by Eq. (1):
(1) CI=15.29 (±1.53)C (L-Lys) + 10.23 (±4.50) (n=3)
where n is the number of used sample. Limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 0.97 mM for L-Lys. LOD was not calculated for D-Lys due to the fact that no significant change was observed at CI for D-Lys.

Understanding the nature of the enantioselectivity at molecular level is of crucial significance to elucidate experimental results. Molecular modeling would provide deeper insights to evaluate the dimensional complementarities.

Taking into account the experimental results, molecular docking was performed by using ADVina. Within this respect, the complexation behavior of possible cCQDs structure with D-/L-Lys was investigated and the conformational sampling of each cysteine-modified edge groups was shown in Fig. S3. In addition, we showed possible structure of cCQDs and the conformational modeling for the total interaction between D-Lys or L-Lys enantiomers and cCQDs in Fig. 8 .

Correspondingly, it is interesting to express that chirality of Lys enantiomers and cCQDs implemented different dimensional complementarities in docking modes upon complexation. Gibbs free energies manifesting the strength of the complexation between the edge groups of cCQDs and D-/L-Lys were calculated (Fig.S4) and given in Table 1 . It should be remarked that a significant difference was observed for the interaction of cCQDs with L-Lys compared to D-Lys.

In this study, we propose a combination of cCQDs and nanopaper that is expected to be a perfect couple for photoluminescent sensors due to their superior physical properties. The proposed nanopaper-based photoluminescent sensor showed different enantioselective response toward L-Lys among the tested enantiomers of amino acids due to conformational difference. In addition, this phenomena was elucidated by molecular modeling studies. Within this respect, this study is the first use of a cCQDs-based nanopaper for enantiosensing applications. We foresee that combination of cCQDs and nanopaper opens up new avenues to develop novel enantiosensing strategies which can be readily analyzed by camera supported devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We express our deep thanks to the Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TÜBİTAK) for financial support (215Z222).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.10.026.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
